title: React Native Core Components Quiz
description: Test your knowledge on View, Text, ScrollView, and FlatList.

questions.question: In React Native, what happens physically 'under the hood' when you utilize a primitive `<View>` component in your JavaScript code?

options.text: It compiles down into a generic HTML `<div>` inside an embedded WebView browser.
React Native uses a Bridge to translate the generic `<View>` down into a true, native OS element (a `UIView` on iOS and `ViewGroup` on Android) at runtime.
It runs the Javascript physically on the device's CPU.
It creates a canvas element and draws pixels manually.

questions.question: What happens if you attempt to render a raw string like `Hello World` directly inside a `<View>` without wrapping it in a `<Text>` component?

options.text: It renders perfectly just like in a web browser using React.js.
It renders, but the font size is extremely tiny.
The mobile application will immediately crash, throwing a strict invariant violation error.
It defaults to black text with a white background.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a crucial and unique requirement for rendering remote images (from a URL) using the `<Image>` component that differs from traditional web development?

options.text: The image must end strictly in `.png` format.
You must physically define static `width` and `height` style properties on the component, or the image fundamentally will not render.
You must base64 encode the URL string.
You must use an `` tag instead.

questions.question: If your application requires rendering a massive database list containing 5,000 distinct items (like a Twitter feed), which component MUST you use to prevent Severe memory leaks and crashes?

options.text: A standard `<View>`.
A `<ScrollView>`, because it handles unlimited data gracefully.
A `<FlatList>`, because it implements 'windowing', only physically rendering the tiny subset of items actively visible on the phone screen at one time.
An HTML `<map>` function.

questions.question: You place three `<Text>` components inside a `<View>`. The text exceeds the physical height of the phone screen, but the user cannot swipe down to view the hidden text. Why?

options.text: Because they have not authenticated via FaceID.
Because standard `<View>` components are fundamentally static containers and do NOT possess scrolling capabilities natively. The content must be explicitly wrapped in a `<ScrollView>`.
Because the text is absolute positioned.
Because the app is running in Development mode.
